messages.content: can I join the two tables in qliksense sheet

Yes, you can join two tables in Qlik Sense sheet. To join two tables, follow these steps:

1. Load the two tables in Qlik Sense.
2. Create a new sheet in Qlik Sense.
3. Go to the "Data" tab and select the first table you want to join.
4. Click on the "Join" button at the top and select the second table you want to join.
5. Choose the fields you want to use as the key fields for the join.
6. Select the type of join you want to perform (inner, left, right, etc.).
7. Click "Apply" to join the two tables.
8. Now you can create visualizations using the joined data.
I can not see the data tab

If you are unable to see the "Data" tab in Qlik Sense, it may be because you don't have the necessary permissions to access the data load editor. In Qlik Sense, only users with the appropriate security rules can access data load editor.

If you believe you have the necessary permissions, try opening the sheet in edit mode and then clicking on the "View" tab. From there, select "Data model viewer" to access the data model. Alternatively, you can use the "Edit Script" option on the "View" tab to directly access and modify the script that loads your data.

If you are still unable to access the data in Qlik Sense, you may need to contact your system administrator to adjust your user permissions or resolve any other configuration issues.
I can see data load editor

If you can see the data load editor but cannot see the data tab in Qlik Sense, it's possible that you may not have any data loaded into your app yet. You need to first load data into Qlik Sense before you can see it in the data tab.

To load data into Qlik Sense, follow these steps:
1. Open the data load editor by clicking on "Edit Script" from the "View" menu in the Qlik Sense hub.
2. In the data load script, specify the data source, i.e., where the data is located and how it's formatted.
3. Write the load statement(s) that will load the data into Qlik Sense. It's important to ensure that the load statements are correct and can successfully load the data.
4. Save the script.
5. Reload the data into your app by clicking on "Load Data" from the "Data Load Editor" window. When the data is loaded, you should be able to see it in the Data tab.

If the data tab is still not visible after loading data, you may need to check your user permissions or contact your system administrator.
I can see the tables drop down from filter by table

If you can see the tables drop-down menu from the "Filter by Table" option in Qlik Sense, but still cannot see the individual data fields in those tables, it's possible that the fields are not loaded or aggregated properly in the app.

You can try the following steps to fix this issue:

1. Go to the "Data Load Editor" in Qlik Sense.
2. Check if all required fields are loaded into the app or not. Make sure that the fields are loaded with the correct data type.
3. Check if the fields have any duplicate names or conflicting data types.
4. If there are any issues with the data loading process, modify the script accordingly.
5. Reload the data after modifying the script. 

Once the data is loaded correctly, you should be able to see the individual fields in the tables drop-down menu. If the issue still persists, then there might be other issues that need to be resolved by analyzing your data and the application in more detail.
I can see the tables in that drop down

If you can see the tables in the drop-down menu when using the "Filter by Table" option in Qlik Sense, but not the fields within those tables, it's possible that the fields are hidden or not yet added to the data model. 

To add the fields to the analysis, you can try the following steps:

1. Go to the "Data Manager" in Qlik Sense and select the table with the missing fields.
2. Check if the missing fields are hidden using the "Hidden Fields" drop-down option. If so, unhide the fields to see them in the analysis. 
3. If the fields are not hidden, make sure that they are properly loaded in the data model. You can do this by navigating to the "Data Load Editor" and checking the load script. If the fields are not present in the load statement, add them in the appropriate location and reload the data. 
4. Once the fields are properly loaded, go back to the analysis and refresh your view to see the fields. To refresh the analysis, you can click on "Reload" or simply refresh the page.

If the above steps do not work, there may be other issues with your data or application that require further investigation.
I can see the fields

If you can see the fields when using the "Filter by Table" option in Qlik Sense, then it should be possible to include those fields in your visualization or analysis.

To include those fields in a visualization, you can follow these steps:

1. Open a new sheet or an existing sheet in Qlik Sense.
2. Drag and drop the table with the fields you want to include into the canvas area.
3. Choose the appropriate chart type for your analysis.
4. Drag and drop the fields from the table into the appropriate areas of the chart, such as the dimensions or measures.
5. Adjust any settings or formatting options as needed for your visualization.
6. Save your chart and add it to your story or sheet.

Repeat these steps as needed for your other tables and fields. By using Qlik Sense's drag-and-drop interface, you can easily customize and create visualizations to explore and analyze your data.
how can I join these two tables now

To join two tables in Qlik Sense, you can use the "Join" option available in the data load editor. Follow these steps:

1. Open the data load editor in Qlik Sense.
2. From the left-hand panel, select the first table you want to join.
3. Click on the "Join" icon at the top of the screen.
4. Select the second table you want to join from the drop-down menu.
5. Choose the fields you want to join on (these should be common fields between the two tables).
6. Select the type of join you want to perform, for example, left join or inner join.
7. Click "Apply" to create the join.
8. Review the data model to ensure the data is being joined as expected.
9. When the join is complete, you can save your changes and reload the data for your app.

Once the data is loaded, you can create visualizations that take into account the joined data. You can access the joined fields in the data model or create new visualizations or measures that reference the combined data.
I can not see the Join icon

If you cannot see the "Join" icon in Qlik Sense's data load editor, it could be because you are using an older version of Qlik Sense that doesn't support join functionality. Join functionality was introduced in Qlik Sense version 3.0. Be sure to check that your version is at least 3.0 or higher to use this feature.

If you are using a version of Qlik Sense 3.0 or higher and still cannot see the "Join" icon, you may need to ensure that you have the necessary permissions to perform data modeling tasks. In Qlik Sense, different users may have different access rights and privileges, which can affect their ability to perform certain tasks within the app, such as joining tables.

You can check your access rights by going to the "Security Rules" or "Data Load Editor" section of the Qlik Sense admin console or by contacting your system administrator. Ensure that you have been granted access to data modeling functionality, which includes the ability to join tables.
is there any way i can join on these two tables when I have to get average exception time from It's creation time in one table and done date in second table. they both table has common key

Yes, you can join two tables and calculate the average exception time from the creation time in one table and done date in the second table.

To do this, you would need to bring both tables together using the common key and calculate the difference in time between the creation date and the done date for each record. You can then calculate the average exception time across all records.

Here are the detailed steps:

1. Open the data load editor in Qlik Sense.
2. Load both tables into the data model.
3. Join the tables based on a common key using the "Join" statement in the script editor.
4. Create a new field called "Exception Time" using the following expression: 

   ```
   (done_date - creation_time) / (60*60*24) AS [Exception Time]
   ```

   This will calculate the difference in time between the creation time and done date and convert it to days.
5. Calculate the average exception time using the "Avg" aggregation function. For example:

   ```
   AVG([Exception Time]) AS [Avg Exception Time]
   ```

   This will calculate the average exception time across all records.

6. Save the script and reload the data.

Once you have completed these steps, you can use the new field "Avg Exception Time" as a measure in your visualizations to see the average exception time across the combined data. You can also use other fields from both tables to create more complex visualizations.
what is 606024

606024 can represent different values or meanings depending on the context in which it is used. 

One possibility is that it represents a duration of time in seconds. For example, 606024 seconds is equal to 7 days, 0 hours, and 50 minutes. This could be used in a variety of applications, such as measuring uptime or downtime of a system, or tracking the duration of an event.

Another possibility is that it is part of a sequence or code that has a specific meaning in a certain context, such as in reference to a product or item in an inventory system. The meaning of "606024" in this context would depend on the code system being used, the industry, and other factors.

Without additional information or context, it is difficult to determine the specific meaning of "606024" in a given situation.
I have creation date in dd/mm/yyyy format and done date in yyyy-mm-dd hh:ss:

To calculate the difference in time between a creation date in dd/mm/yyyy format and a done date in yyyy-mm-dd hh:ss format, you will need to convert the dates to a common format and then calculate the time difference between them.

Here are the steps to do this:

1. In the data load script, use the Date#() function to convert the creation date in the dd/mm/yyyy format to a date field.
 
   ```
    Date#(creation_date, 'DD/MM/YYYY') as creation_date
   ```

2. In the data load script, use the Timestamp#() function to convert the done date in the yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss format to a date and time field.

   ```
    Timestamp#(done_date, 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss') as done_date
   ```

3. Join the two tables based on the common field/key using a Join statement. 

4. Create a new field called "Exception Time" using the following expression:

   ```
   ((done_date - creation_date)*86400) AS [Exception Time in Seconds]
   ```

   This will calculate the difference in time between the creation time and done date and convert it to seconds using the number of seconds in a day (86400).

5. Calculate the average exception time using the "Avg" aggregation function. For example:

   ```
   AVG([Exception Time in Seconds]) AS [Avg Exception Time in Seconds]
   ```

   This will calculate the average exception time across all records in seconds.

6. Save the script and reload the data.

Once you have completed these steps, you will be able to use the new field "Avg Exception Time in Seconds" as a measure in your visualizations to see the average exception time across the combined data in seconds. You can also convert the time measurement to any other time unit as per your requirement.
why are you using 86400

In step 4 when calculating the "Exception Time" in seconds, we multiply the time difference by the number of seconds in a day (86400). 

The reason for using 86400 is because the difference between two dates and times is usually expressed in seconds. Since there are 24 hours in a day and 60 minutes in an hour, we can convert the number of days in the time difference to seconds by multiplying by the number of seconds in a day, which is 86400.

So, in order to convert the time difference to seconds, we need to multiply the difference by 86400 to get the time difference in seconds instead of days.

For example, if the time difference between the two dates is one day, then the value of "Exception Time" will be 86400 (1 day * 86400 seconds). Similarly, if the time difference is one hour, then the value of "Exception Time" will be 3600 (1 hour * 60 minutes * 60 seconds).
calculate it in hrs

To calculate the average exception time in hours instead of seconds, you can modify the "Exception Time" expression that we used earlier to convert the time difference to hours. 

Instead of multiplying the time difference by 86400, we can divide it by 3600 (the number of seconds in an hour) to get the time difference in hours. Here are the steps to modify the calculation:

1. In the data load script, use the Date#() function to convert the creation date in the dd/mm/yyyy format to a date field.

   ```
    Date#(creation_date, 'DD/MM/YYYY') as creation_date
   ```

2. In the data load script, use the Timestamp#() function to convert the done date in the yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm:ss format to a date and time field.

   ```
    Timestamp#(done_date, 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss') as done_date
   ```

3. Join the two tables based on the common field/key using a Join statement.
 
4. Create a new field called "Exception Time" using the following expression:

   ```
   ((done_date - creation_date)*24) AS [Exception Time in Hours]
   ```

   This will calculate the difference in time between the creation time and done date and convert it to hours (multiplying with 24).

5. Calculate the average exception time in hours using the "Avg" aggregation function. For example:

   ```
   AVG([Exception Time in Hours]) AS [Avg Exception Time in Hours]
   ```

   This will calculate the average exception time across all records in hours.

6. Save the script and reload the data.

Once you have completed these steps, you will be able to use the new field "Avg Exception Time in Hours" as a measure in your visualizations to see the average exception time across the combined data in hours.
can you calculate this when key from table = key from another table

Yes, you can calculate the average exception time when joining two tables based on a common key field. Here are the steps:

1. Open the data load editor in Qlik Sense.
2. Load both tables into the data model.
3. Join the tables based on a common key field using the "Join" statement in the script editor. The syntax of the join statement would be:

   ```
   JOIN (Table1)
   LOAD
    (key_field),
    (creation_date)
   RESIDENT (Table2);
   ```

   replace Table1 with the first table, Table2 with the second table, key_field with the common key field on which to join the tables, and creation_date with the creation date field in Table2.

4. Create a new field called "Exception Time" using the following expression:
   ```
   (done_date - creation_date) AS [Exception Time]
   ```

   This will calculate the difference in time between the creation time and done date.

5. Calculate the average exception time using the "Avg" aggregation function. For example:

   ```
   AVG([Exception Time]) AS [Avg Exception Time]
   ```

   This will calculate the average exception time across all records.

6. Save the script and reload the data.

Once you have completed these steps, you will be able to use the new field "Avg Exception Time" as a measure in your visualizations to see the average exception time across the combined data.

Note that in the above steps, we assumed that the key field and creation date fields are present in different tables and you need to join both tables based on the key field. If your scenario is different, where key and creation date fields are present in the same table, you need to adjust the syntax of the join statement accordingly.
It's unable to fine the uncommon fields from the first table even though they exists and have correct name

If you cannot find the uncommon fields in the first table, there could be several reasons for this issue. Here are a few possible causes and solutions:

1. Check if the fields are correctly loaded:
   Verify that the uncommon fields are correctly loaded from the data source. Sometimes, data may contain errors, and values may not correspond to the expected data type. You may need to check the data source and verify that the fields exist and have the correct name and data type.

2. Try renaming the field:
   It's possible that the field name has been changed or is not matching the original name. You can try making sure that the field names are consistent between the data model and the visualizations. You may also try renaming the field to a different name and see if it appears in the visualizations.

3. Check for hidden fields:
   It is possible that the uncommon fields are hidden from the visualization. You should check whether the fields are hidden from the data model or the visualization. You can unhide the fields by using the "Hidden Fields" option from the "Fields" window.

4. Check for field-level permissions:
   Check whether the fields are restricted by any field-level security rules that may be preventing them from appearing in the visualization. You may need to modify the field-level security rules to include the required fields.

If none of the above solutions works, then you need to further investigate the issue by checking the data model, data source, or any other settings in Qlik Sense to determine the root cause of the issue.
it's just doing the join on the same table not on two different tables
